skill.name: clean-code
skill.description: Applies principles from Robert C. Martin's 'Clean Code'. Use this skill when writing, reviewing, or refactoring code to ensure high quality, readability, and maintainability. Covers naming, functions, comments, formatting, objects, error handling, testing, and class design.
skill.summary: Transform working code into clean, maintainable code using industry-standard principles
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected 29 potential issues (external commands, network patterns, weak cryptography, system reconnaissance), but all are FALSE POSITIVES. The findings match Markdown code block delimiters (backticks), documentation examples, and reference URLs in educational content. The skill contains no executable code, performs no file operations, makes no network requests, and is purely documentation about software engineering principles. Safe to publish.

content.user_title: Apply Clean Code Principles to Your Codebase
content.value_statement: Write code that works AND is easy to maintain. This skill applies Robert C. Martin's Clean Code principles to transform messy code into readable, testable, and professional software that other developers can understand and modify.
actual_capabilities: Applies Clean Code principles from Robert C. Martin's methodology
Reviews code for naming conventions, function design, and structure
Identifies code smells and suggests refactoring improvements
Provides guidance on comment usage and when code should be self-documenting
Enforces Single Responsibility Principle and object-oriented design patterns
Suggests test-driven development practices and FIRST principles
limitations: Cannot automatically refactor code without explicit user request
Does not execute automated testing frameworks or test runners
Cannot guarantee performance improvements from refactoring
May suggest changes that require significant restructuring of existing code
Principles are guidelines, not absolute rules for every situation

use_cases.title: Pull Request Code Review
use_cases.description: Apply Clean Code principles during PR reviews to provide constructive, principle-based feedback on naming, function size, and code organization before merging.

use_cases.title: Legacy Code Refactoring
use_cases.description: Transform difficult-to-maintain legacy code into clean, readable code by identifying code smells, breaking large functions into smaller ones, and improving naming conventions.

use_cases.title: New Feature Development
use_cases.description: Write clean code from the start when implementing new features, ensuring functions are small, names are meaningful, and code follows industry best practices.

prompt_templates.title: Review This Function
prompt_templates.prompt:
Review this function against Clean Code principles. Check if it does one thing, has a descriptive name, appropriate number of arguments, and is small enough. Suggest improvements:

[PASTE CODE]
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick code review of a single function

prompt_templates.title: Refactor This Code
prompt_templates.prompt:
Apply Clean Code principles to refactor this code. Focus on: meaningful names, small functions, one level of abstraction, removing bad comments, and proper error handling. Explain each change:

[PASTE CODE]

prompt_templates.title: Identify Code Smells
prompt_templates.prompt:
Analyze this codebase/file for code smells including: rigidity, fragility, immobility, viscosity, needless complexity, and repetition. List specific issues with line numbers:

[PASTE CODE]
prompt_templates.scenario: Identifying technical debt and code quality issues

prompt_templates.title: Improve Naming Conventions
prompt_templates.prompt:
Review the names in this code (variables, functions, classes) for intention-revealing clarity, pronounceability, and searchability. Suggest better names and explain why:

[PASTE CODE]
prompt_templates.scenario: Improving code readability through better naming

output_examples.input: Review this function: def chk(u): if u.age>65 and u.tp=='h': return True
output_examples.output: Found 3 Clean Code violations: 1) Function name 'chk' is not intention-revealing. Suggest: isEligibleForFullBenefits. 2) Parameter 'u' is not searchable. Suggest: employee. 3) Magic comparison 'tp==h' uses unclear abbreviation. Extract to isHourly() method or use named constant. Refactored version provided with explanation.

output_examples.input: Apply Clean Code principles to this 50-line function that does validation, database save, sends email, and logs metrics
output_examples.output: This function violates the Single Responsibility Principle. Split into 4 functions: 1) validateUserCredentials(), 2) saveUserToDatabase(), 3) sendWelcomeEmail(), 4) logUserMetrics(). Each function should be under 10 lines. The main function orchestrates these steps with clear error handling at each stage.

best_practices: Use the skill during code reviews to provide objective, principle-based feedback rather than subjective opinions
Apply Clean Code principles incrementally when refactoring—don't try to fix everything at once
Focus on the most impactful improvements first: function size, naming, and eliminating code duplication
Balance clean code principles with practical constraints like deadlines and team context
anti_patterns: Don't blindly apply all principles without considering context—sometimes a 3-argument function is the clearest solution
Avoid commenting every line of code—strive for self-documenting code that needs no explanation
Don't refactor without tests—ensure you have test coverage before making structural changes
Never sacrifice correctness for cleanliness—code must work before it can be clean

faq.question: Should I apply all Clean Code principles all the time?
faq.answer: No. Clean Code principles are guidelines, not absolute rules. Apply them pragmatically based on context, team standards, and project constraints. Focus on the most impactful improvements: clear naming, small functions, and single responsibility.

faq.question: Can this skill automatically refactor my code?
faq.answer: No. This skill provides analysis, suggestions, and refactoring examples based on Clean Code principles. You must review the suggestions and apply them yourself. Always test refactored code to ensure behavior is preserved.

faq.question: What's the most important Clean Code principle?
faq.answer: The Single Responsibility Principle—that functions and classes should do one thing well. This leads to smaller, more focused, testable, and maintainable code. Most other Clean Code principles flow from this core idea.

faq.question: Does this skill replace the need for code reviews?
faq.answer: No. This skill enhances code reviews by providing principle-based feedback, but human reviewers bring context, domain knowledge, and team-specific considerations that AI cannot fully replicate.

faq.question: Will following these principles make my code slower?
faq.answer: Typically no. Clean code tends to be more modular and testable, which can make optimization easier. However, micro-optimizations sometimes conflict with clean code. Prioritize cleanliness first, optimize only when measurements show it's needed.

faq.question: Can I use this skill with any programming language?
faq.answer: Yes. Clean Code principles apply to all programming languages. While specific examples in the skill may use certain languages, the underlying concepts—meaningful names, small functions, clear abstractions—are universal across programming languages.
